speaker: interviewer
text: Kathy Levy-- and this is tape number 3. We've heard how you came back to Budapest after your illness in Theresienstadt. Could you firstly say whether the illness had left you completely by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. I was all right. Listen, when one is 19 years old, or 20 years old, you recover from everything easier than when you are older. So I recovered quite well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, who had been in the safe-keeping of Raoul Wallenberg and his organization, did she speak a lot about what had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they, they had no idea what was going on. They knew very little about Wallenberg, and who he was, and what happened. They only knew that they were taken from the ghetto to these safe houses. And that's where they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your mother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- we had no idea where my father was. And two weeks after I came home-- I remember on a Sunday morning-- we were in the flat at home where we lived, and the bell went. And I went to the door. And who was standing at the door, but my father and my uncle? They just came back. We had no idea where he was. And they didn't know when to expect him. Two weeks after me, they arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had he spent all his time at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchenwald and Gelsenkirchen. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that must have been a great reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a bit about your life after that. What did your father do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father actually went back to his old job after the war. He was taken back to his old position and his old job. And he was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I went back, and I was working for-- my mother had a-- no, no, that was before the war. After the war. No. I went back, and I was speaking about my uncle, who lived in Scotland. And he was writing letters, why don't you let-- I was called Katush in Hungary at home. Why don't you let Katush come to stay with us? I have got two little boys, and she can stay here and look after my children, and so on, and so forth. So in 1940-- end of 1945, December, I came to Scotland. I got passport and a visa, and I came to Scotland.

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin, whom I was speaking about earlier, she survived the war in Budapest. And she had relations in London. So we traveled together to this country. She stayed in London, and I came up to Scotland. And in those days, my uncle had a farm in Cumbernauld, outside Cumbernauld. And I lived on that farm for a year. I was here for a year, staying with them. But I wasn't very fond of the farm life. I must admit. And after a year, when my papers ran out, I went back to Hungary. And then I went-- I got an office job. And in 1952, I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest. I married my first husband. And in 1954, I had my daughter. And when my daughter was about a year old, my husband became ill. He had multiple sclerosis. And unfortunately, very shortly after, he passed away in 1955-- '56. Just before the Hungarian Revolution, he passed away. And then 1956 came the Hungarian Revolution. And by then, my uncle had a big business in Glasgow. He wasn't on the farm anymore. And he has written again. And he said, now the whole family can come. I can give you a job. I have got a house you can live in. And so my grandmother, my parents, my little daughter, and me, we came to Glasgow. We lived in the same house on the farm. But by then, there was no farm anymore. But lived in Cumbernauld-- Eastfield Cumbernauld-- with my family. And my father and I was working in the shop for my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Starting in 1956?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it had been 11 years since you had been to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Scotland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your first husband's illness arise in any way as a result of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. You see, there are so many, unfortunately, of these cases. You can hear about it now as well. So I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to live under the Communist regime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't a pleasure. No pleasure at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: No freedom.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But plenty of food and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. There was enough of everything. We weren't starving, but it wasn't a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Returning to your arrival in England in 1956, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working with him in the shop for a while. And then somebody suggested to me, because I was always very good with my hands-- I mean, sewing and-- I did in school-- I went to a special school where we did dress making and things like that. And somebody said to me that, why don't you go to college and become a domestic science teacher? I said, I can't be a domestic science teacher because I never did any physics. Oh, but you can be a domestic science-- a sewing teacher. So I did that part of training. I became a domestic science teacher, but I only did the sewing part of it. And three years I went to college. I got a grant so that I could keep my child. By then, we moved into Glasgow, with my mother, and father, and my grandmother. And I could keep my daughter on my grant and myself. And I qualified, and then I started teaching. I was teaching for two years in the Govan High School in Glasgow, until I got married. And that's the end of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a bit about your second marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not bad. It's all right. We were-- we, we met here. And our background was completely different because I don't come from a very Orthodox family as my husband. But I learned it. And I became the way he wanted it. And we have got lots of lovely friends in Glasgow. And we are living very happily there. And the greatest happiness was when I had my son. Although, I wasn't a baby by then. And I never thought that I will have another child. But that was the, the biggest gift of God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- just to finish off, would you, just perhaps sum up, or have a message, or something that you would like to say in conclusion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's hard work to do these interviews for the interviewer and the interviewee. But I feel it's a very, very important thing because it's most important that the new generation should know and should learn about it, what we have gone through. And that should never, ever happen again, what happened in the '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: The interview with Kathy Levy is concluded. There are no-- there's no interview with her family because that interview has taken place when her husband, the Reverend Ernest Levy, was interviewed on a separate tape. Photographs and the family are on that tape. Thank you.
